analogy: Think of "Natural Vegetation and Wildlife" through the chapter's own opening images: Shrikant Uncle was home after a gap of nearly four months. He was a wildlife photographer and travelled widely. Renuka’s interest in wildlife and forests began at an early age, when her uncle introduced her to books on nature. Pictures of distant lands and people,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 40 OUR ENVIRONMENT, LIFE IN THE AMAZON BASIN, HUMAN ENVIRONMENT INTERACTIONS: THE TROPICAL AND THE SUBTROPICAL REGION 41, CLIMATE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Natural Vegetation and Wildlife is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that shrikant Uncle was home after a gap of nearly four months.

The text explains that he was a wildlife photographer and travelled widely.

The text explains that renuka’s interest in wildlife and forests began at an early age, when her uncle introduced her to books on nature.

The text explains that pictures of distant lands and people, who lived there, always fascinated her.

The text explains that 6.1: People from various parts of the world “In these pictures Renuka, you can see people from different parts of the world – some from dry deserts, some from frozen lands and some from hot wet rainforests.” “They look so different from me”, observed Renuka. “They may look different, but they share the same basic needs of life – food, clothing and shelter”, explained Shrikant Uncle. “Their children do the same things as you probably do, play games, quarrel sometimes and then make-up, sing, dance and help the families with various things that need to be done.

The text explains that they live closer to nature and very early in their lives have learnt to care for nature.

The text explains that they learn how to catch fish and how to collect material from the forests.”

The text explains that 40 OUR ENVIRONMENT Glossary LIFE IN THE AMAZON BASIN Before learning about the Amazon basin, let us look at the map (Fig.

The text explains that notice that the tropical region lies very close to the equator; between 10°N and 10°S.

The text explains that so, it is referred to as the equatorial region.

The text explains that notice how it flows from the mountains to the west and reaches the Atlantic Ocean to the east.

The text explains that the place where a river flows into another body of water is called the river’s mouth.

The text explains that numerous tributaries join the Amazon River to form the Amazon basin.

The text explains that the river basin drains portions of Brazil, parts of Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, Columbia and a small part of Venezuela.

The text explains that name the countries of the basin through which the equator passes.

The text explains that tributaries: These are small rivers that join the main river.

The text explains that hUMAN ENVIRONMENT INTERACTIONS: THE TROPICAL AND THE SUBTROPICAL REGION 41 CLIMATE As you now know, the Amazon Basin stretches directly on the equator and is characterized by hot and wet climate throughout the year.

The text explains that the day temperatures are high with very high humidity.

The text explains that at night the temperature goes down but the humidity remains high.

The text explains that 44 OUR ENVIRONMENT Glossary Himalayas and the Sundarbans delta are the main features of this basin.

The text explains that the density of population of the plains is very high.

The text explains that population density: It means the number of persons that live in one sq. km. of area e.g. the population density of Uttarakhand is 189 while the density of West Bengal is 1029 and that of Bihar is 1102.

The text explains that 6.11 : One horned rhinoceros Lake: A source of livelihood (A case study) Binod is a fisherman living in the Matwali Maun village of Bihar.

The text explains that (i) On an outline map of the Indian Sub-continent, draw the rivers Ganga and Brahmaputra from the source to the mouth.

The text explains that press the soil to make it level with the top of the can.

The text explains that now put four coins or bottle caps on the soil of each can.

The text explains that take the big can that has been punched with holes and fill it with water.

The text explains that you can also take the sprinkling can from your garden.

The text explains that on the first can sprinkle water very slowly so that no soil splashes out.

The text explains that let moderate amount of water be sprinkled on the second can.

The text explains that on the third can, sprinkle the water heavily.

The text explains that you will observe that unprotected soil splashes out.

The text explains that where the ‘rain’ is heavy the amount of soil that splashes out is the maximum and least in case of the first can.

The text explains that it is clear that if the land is cleared completely of the vegetation, the soil cover will quickly disappear.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Environment, Chapter 6: Natural Vegetation and Wildlife

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
